did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses an ensemble deep learning framework combining InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2 architectures for automated skin cancer classification. It was evaluated on the ISIC 2018 dermoscopic image dataset, achieving 95% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel ensemble deep learning framework (InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2) for skin cancer diagnosis. It includes a methodology section, experimental evaluation on the ISIC 2018 dataset, comparative results against state-of-the-art methods, and an ablation study. Although it contains a literature survey section, its primary purpose is reporting new technical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Towards Improved Skin Cancer Diagnosis:A Reliable Predictive Framework
journal: Proceedings of the 5th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN-2025)
brief_summary: This study proposes a novel ensemble deep learning framework combining InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2 architectures to improve the accuracy and reliability of automated skin cancer classification. The model was evaluated on the ISIC 2018 dermoscopic image dataset, which includes seven distinct skin lesion categories, and utilized targeted data augmentation to address class imbalance. Experimental results demonstrated that the proposed method achieved a classification accuracy of 95%, outperforming state-of-the-art models such as EfficientNetB4 (87.91%) and DarkNet-53 (85.4%). The framework also recorded strong performance metrics, including an average precision, recall, and F1-score of 0.94, 0.94, and 0.93, respectively. These findings validate the robustness and generalizability of the ensemble approach for dermatological diagnostics. The authors suggest the lightweight architecture is suitable for real-world clinical and telemedicine deployment, with future work focusing on interpretability and computational efficiency.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the 7 distinct classes in the ISIC 2018 dataset used for classification; 149 images in the dataset.
One of the 7 distinct classes in the ISIC 2018 dataset used for classification; 622 images in the dataset.
One of the 7 distinct classes in the ISIC 2018 dataset used for classification; 160 images in the dataset.
One of the 7 distinct classes in the ISIC 2018 dataset used for classification; 1,305 images in the dataset. Also highlighted in the abstract and introduction as a major global health concern.
One of the 7 distinct classes in the ISIC 2018 dataset used for classification; 7,737 images in the dataset (most frequent class).
One of the 7 distinct classes in the ISIC 2018 dataset used for classification; 1,338 images in the dataset.
One of the 7 distinct classes in the ISIC 2018 dataset used for classification; 180 images in the dataset.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin lesion categories (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of skin lesions using an ensemble deep learning framework (InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2) on the ISIC 2018 dataset. The model uses a Softmax activation function for multi-class classification and Sparse Categorical Cross-Entropy loss. It achieves 95% accuracy, with precision, recall, and F1-score of 0.94, 0.94, and 0.93 respectively.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A publicly available benchmark dataset containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. It is described as a vast collection of malignant and benign skin condition images with varying resolutions, lighting conditions, and noise artifacts.

use.training: Used for training the ensemble deep learning framework (InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2) with data augmentation (random rotations, translations, zooming, flipping) to mitigate class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (196 images).
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation (1,515 images).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate the statistical significance of the accuracy improvement of the proposed ensemble model compared to previous state-of-the-art methods.
To determine statistical significance for the paired t-test results.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no statistically significant difference in classification accuracy between the proposed InceptionV3-MobileNetV2 ensemble model and previous state-of-the-art methods.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: The proposed InceptionV3-MobileNetV2 ensemble model has a statistically significant improvement in classification accuracy compared to previous state-of-the-art methods.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paired t-test confirmed that the accuracy gain of the proposed model over previous state-of-the-art methods is statistically significant (p < 0.05), reinforcing the robustness of the proposed methodology.

ISIC 2018.notes: The proposed ensemble model achieved 95% accuracy on the ISIC 2018 test set (1,515 images). It outperformed EfficientNetB4 (87.91%), EL-DLOA (88%), DarkNet-53/DenseNet-201 (85.4%), and EVC-CCPA Residual Block (92.12%). Statistical significance was confirmed via paired t-test (p < 0.05). Class 6 achieved perfect recall (1.00), while Class 3 had the lowest recall (0.70). Validation accuracy stabilized at 95.00% by epoch 11.

XJUSK.notes: The EVC-CCPA Residual Block model achieved 86% accuracy on the XJUSK dataset, as cited in comparative results.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning using weights pre-trained on ImageNet

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Hierarchical feature extraction from both models; lower layers detect edges/textures, deeper layers detect complex structures like asymmetry and pigmentation
architecture_details.fusion_method: Concatenation of extracted feature vectors (F_lnc and F_Alob) to produce a deep feature vector
architecture_details.classifier_head: Fully connected classification network with Softmax activation for multi-class classification

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected network with Softmax activation

preprocessing_techniques: Handling missing or corrupt image data by identification and removal
Min-max normalization of pixel intensity values to range [0, 1]
Resizing all images to fixed dimensions of 224x224 pixels

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were randomly rotated within the range θ ∈ [−20°, 20°] to ensure robustness to variations in lesion positioning.
Scaling was applied within the factor S ∈ [0.8, 1.2] to introduce changes in lesion size and improve adaptability at various magnifications.
Horizontal and vertical mirroring was performed to alleviate spatial invariance and allow the model to recognize lesions from any orientation.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Targeted Data Augmentation for Class Imbalance
data_augmentation_techniques.details: The aforementioned geometric transforms were employed specifically to mitigate class imbalance by artificially increasing the representation of minority classes (e.g., Actinic Keratosis, Vascular Lesions) and ensuring a well-distributed dataset.

conclusions: The proposed ensemble deep learning framework combining InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2 effectively leverages complementary local and global feature extraction capabilities to improve skin cancer classification accuracy.

The model achieved a classification accuracy of 95% on the ISIC 2018 dataset, outperforming state-of-the-art methods such as EfficientNetB4 (87.91%), EL-DLOA (88%), and DarkNet-53/DenseNet-201 (85.4%).
The framework demonstrated strong performance metrics with an average precision of 0.94, recall of 0.94, and F1-score of 0.93, validating its robustness and generalizability.
Statistical significance analysis using a paired t-test confirmed that the accuracy improvement of the proposed model over previous state-of-the-art methods is statistically significant (p < 0.05).
Targeted data augmentation strategies successfully mitigated class imbalance within the dataset, improving model robustness.
The framework shows promising potential for real-world clinical and telemedicine deployment due to its balance of high accuracy and computational efficiency.
Future research directions include enhancing interpretability through explainable AI, expanding dataset diversity, and optimizing computational efficiency via model compression.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset for evaluation, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., 7,737 Nevus images vs. 149 Actinic Keratosis images), potentially biasing performance metrics towards majority classes.

External validation is limited; while the paper cites an 86% accuracy on the XJUSK dataset for a comparative model (EVC-CCPA), it does not report performance for the proposed ensemble model on any external or independent dataset.

The claim of suitability for 'real-time deployment' and 'telemedicine' is speculative, as the paper does not report inference latency, throughput, or hardware-specific deployment benchmarks, only training infrastructure (A100 GPU).

The paper acknowledges that future work is needed to address interpretability (via Explainable AI) and dataset diversity, indicating current limitations in clinical trust and generalizability to broader populations.

Data leakage risk exists if the 'targeted data augmentation' for class imbalance was not strictly confined to the training fold, though the paper states augmentation was used to mitigate imbalance in the training set.

The statistical significance test (paired t-test, p < 0.05) compares the proposed model against 'previous state-of-the-art methods' cited in literature rather than against models trained and evaluated on the same data splits in this study, which may not account for variance in experimental conditions.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the ISIC 2018 dermoscopic image dataset for all phases of the experiment. The dataset was split into training (10,018 images), validation (196 images), and testing (1,515 images) sets. Data augmentation was applied to the training set to mitigate class imbalance. The proposed InceptionV3-MobileNetV2 ensemble model was trained on the training split, validated on the validation split, and tested on the testing split. No external or cross-dataset validation was performed for the proposed model; comparisons with other datasets (e.g., XJUSK) were limited to citing results from other studies.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper does not report any cross-dataset validation where the model was trained on one dataset and tested on a different unseen dataset. The model was evaluated exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset. While the paper cites an accuracy of 86% for a different model (EVC-CCPA Residual Block) on the XJUSK dataset, this was not an experiment conducted by the authors on their proposed model.

training.notes: Used for training the InceptionV3-MobileNetV2 ensemble model. Data augmentation (rotation, scaling, flipping) was applied to this set to address class imbalance.

validation.notes: Used for model selection and early stopping during training. Validation accuracy stabilized at 95.00% by epoch 11.

testing.notes: Used for final performance evaluation of the proposed model. The model achieved 95% accuracy on this set.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: InceptionV3 and MobileNetV2 architectures were initialized with pre-trained weights from ImageNet before being fine-tuned on the ISIC 2018 dataset.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Targeted data augmentation (random rotations within [-20, 20] degrees, scaling within [0.8, 1.2], and horizontal/vertical flipping) was applied to the training images to artificially increase the representation of minority classes.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: The ISIC 2018 dataset was split into 10,018 training images, 196 validation images, and 1,515 testing images. The paper also mentions an 80/20 split conceptually in the augmentation section, but the specific counts provided in the dataset description (10018/196/1515) are the definitive split used.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used for testing the proposed model. The test set (1,515 images) was held-out from the ISIC 2018 dataset.

dataset_level_results.notes: Results on the ISIC 2018 test set. Class 6 (Nevus) had perfect recall (1.00), while Class 3 (Basal Cell Carcinoma) had the lowest recall (0.70).
Validation accuracy stabilized at 95.00% by epoch 11. Training accuracy reached 99.13%.

generalizability_notes: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2018 dataset, which exhibits significant class imbalance (e.g., 7,737 Nevus images vs. 149 Actinic Keratosis images).
External validation is limited; the paper does not report performance for the proposed ensemble model on any external or independent dataset.
The claim of suitability for 'real-time deployment' is speculative as inference latency and hardware-specific benchmarks are not reported.
Future work is acknowledged to address interpretability and dataset diversity, indicating current limitations in generalizability to broader populations.
information_not_reported: No external test set results for the proposed model.
No patient-level separation details (likely image-level given the dataset nature).
No specific hyperparameters for the external comparative models (EfficientNetB4, etc.) other than their reported accuracy.

No statistical significance test results for the comparative models against the proposed model on the same data splits (only a paired t-test against 'previous state-of-the-art' is mentioned, which may refer to literature values rather than re-evaluated baselines).
